key_points: Dorms are old but functional; fire alarms extremely sensitive
Curfew enforced — students literally slept in cars when locked out
Gabby dorms unchanged since at least 2012
key_quotes: I went to ORU and the curfew was 11pm; slept in my car when I couldn't get back in time.
I lived in Gabby, graduated in 2012... Gabby dorms still look the same.
Those fire alarms were soooo sensitive, blow dry your hair, flat iron your hair, and/or anything.

key_points: Strict rules heavily limit typical college social activities
Strong community bonds form among students who embrace the mission
Not a party school — almost anything conventionally 'fun' is restricted
key_quotes: Nobody has any fun at them lol.
I have stayed with friends at other party schools and there is so much worse.
It was the best time in my adult life, nothing since has even came close to replacing that fun time period.

key_points: No meaningful data from either source about dining quality
Graduate Center required dress code — pants and collared shirt to enter
Campus culture is structured; even shared spaces have behavioral rules
key_quotes: You had to wear pants and a collared shirt in the GC/LRC and men couldn't have facial hair.
No specific dining complaints surfaced — absence of data is notable.

key_points: Spiritual community provides genuine emotional support for many students
Strict rules and culture clash can create stress for misaligned students
Students who leave describe feeling out of place, not supported
key_quotes: My relationship with God has grown significantly during my time.
I feel completely alone, and I don't know what to do.
ORU isn't perfect and also isn't for everybody. It can be easy to find the negative in anything these days.

key_points: No direct student complaints or praise about financial aid found
Accreditation questioned by prospective students — may affect aid eligibility
School aggressively recruits students, raising questions about enrollment pressure
key_quotes: Does anyone know the legitimacy of these schools in regards to accreditation and general opinion on them?
Add ORU to the list — they want me so bad, it is not even funny.
I was accepted to ORU in 1999 with an 880 SAT. The joke was if you had a pulse you'd get in.

key_points: Low admissions bar — historically accepted nearly anyone with a pulse
Speaking in tongues remains a pivotal academic and spiritual requirement
Academic quality varies wildly by department; concerns raised about specific professors
key_quotes: I was accepted to ORU in 1999 with an 880 SAT. The joke was if you had a pulse you'd get in.
I'm pretty shocked the honor code is still the same and that speaking in tongues is still so pivotal after the transition and scandals of the Roberts.
If Dr Kay (English Dept) is still there, that's a sad situation. She taught the communist manifesto in honors class — it was shocking.

key_points: Honor code unchanged for decades — rigid and slow to evolve
Roberts family scandals loom in institutional history and reputation
Rules enforced strictly; students describe taking them out of context as common
key_quotes: I'm pretty shocked the honor code is still the same and that speaking in tongues is still so pivotal after the transition and scandals of the Roberts.
Many things Jared says in this video lack supporting information and have been taken out of context.
Oral speaking at my graduation was one of the most disappointing experiences of my time at ORU.

key_points: Campus has a distinctive, grand architectural vision — not a generic school
Located in Tulsa, Oklahoma — not a major college city
Campus security is reportedly high and responsive
key_quotes: The ORU made quite a name for itself because of the grand vision of it all much like The Crystal Cathedral.
The security there is high so things like this are quickly resolved.
ORU was tiny back then, with maybe 1,800 students, but I genuinely enjoyed being there.

key_points: ORU grads praised specifically for workplace integrity and ethics
Communication and nursing degrees cited as producing employed graduates
Employer reputation is a real, tangible asset for graduates
key_quotes: I am a recruiter and I love to hire ORU graduates because of their integrity — they are one of the best employees.
My oldest got a communication degree. My oldest landed a good job. We are very happy with the school overall.
Anna Vaughn School of Nursing — I wanted to quit more than once but God, over 10 years later but God.

key_points: Strong employer reputation may justify cost for faith-aligned students
Low academic bar raises questions about degree market value
Aggressive recruiting suggests enrollment concerns — negotiate aid
key_quotes: I was accepted to ORU in 1999 with an 880 SAT. The joke was if you had a pulse you'd get in.
Does anyone know the legitimacy of these schools in regards to accreditation?
I love to hire ORU graduates because of their integrity.

key_points: Intensely faith-forward — spiritual growth is the core student experience
Deeply polarizing: students either love it or leave quickly
Best fit for committed Pentecostal Christians, very wrong fit for everyone else
key_quotes: There is no place like Oral Roberts University and no college can compare.
It clearly wasn't a good fit for you but these things are pretty common for conservative Pentecostals.
ORU is all about what you make it like many things in life.

key_points: **Admissions Standards:** historically accepted almost anyone — degree signal may be weak
**Honor Code:** unchanged for 40+ years including speaking in tongues requirement
**Institutional History:** Roberts family scandals still referenced by alumni as defining disappointments
key_quotes: The joke was if you had a pulse you'd get in.
I'm pretty shocked the honor code is still the same and that speaking in tongues is still so pivotal after the transition and scandals of the Roberts.
Oral speaking at my graduation was one of the most disappointing experiences of my time at ORU.

key_points: Worship and music program produces genuinely talented, career-ready artists
Tight-knit believer community creates lifelong friendships for the right student
Employer trust in ORU grads is real and consistently mentioned unprompted
key_quotes: I am a recruiter and I love to hire ORU graduates because of their integrity — they are one of the best employees.
The friendships, the activities, the experiences I will always treasure the memories. It was the best time in my adult life.
Being obedient to God during a season of life, you have opened up a door for God to use your life to make history.
